edges.description: Loss of CRB1 disrupts the OLM, leading to fragmented adherens junctions, retinal folds, and pseudorosettes. The resulting thickened and abnormally laminated retina is a hallmark of CRB1 retinopathy.
edges.source: Loss of CRB1 at the outer limiting membrane
edges.target: Outer limiting membrane disruption and retinal disorganization

edges.description: CRB1-associated disease can include keratoconus, suggesting a corneal structural consequence in addition to retinal degeneration.
edges.source: Loss of CRB1 at the outer limiting membrane

edges.description: Structural disorganization renders photoreceptors vulnerable to progressive degeneration, with rate and pattern of loss varying by subtype and modifier context.
edges.source: Outer limiting membrane disruption and retinal disorganization
edges.target: Progressive photoreceptor degeneration and visual loss

edges.description: Progressive rod photoreceptor dysfunction produces night blindness in the RP branch of CRB1 disease.
edges.source: Progressive photoreceptor degeneration and visual loss

edges.description: Severe early-onset CRB1 retinal dysfunction can present with nystagmus.
edges.source: Progressive photoreceptor degeneration and visual loss

edges.description: Progressive rod-cone degeneration leads to declining visual acuity across CRB1 retinal dystrophy subtypes.
edges.source: Progressive photoreceptor degeneration and visual loss

edges.description: CRB1 retinal degeneration can produce peripheral pigment migration and nummular pigmentary changes.
edges.source: Progressive photoreceptor degeneration and visual loss

edges.description: CRB1-associated retinal dystrophy is associated with hyperopia in a subset of patients.
edges.source: Progressive photoreceptor degeneration and visual loss

edges.description: CRB1 retinal degeneration can include cystoid macular fluid collections and macular edema.
edges.source: Progressive photoreceptor degeneration and visual loss

edges.description: Progressive CRB1 retinal degeneration includes a macular-predominant dystrophy subtype.
edges.source: Progressive photoreceptor degeneration and visual loss

edges.description: Progressive peripheral photoreceptor loss produces constriction of the peripheral visual field.
edges.source: Progressive photoreceptor degeneration and visual loss

edges.description: Severe CRB1 photoreceptor dysfunction can extinguish electroretinogram responses.
edges.source: Progressive photoreceptor degeneration and visual loss

edges.target: Loss of CRB1 at the outer limiting membrane

evidence.snippet: Mutations in the CRB1 gene are associated with variable phenotypes of severe retinal dystrophies, ranging from leber congenital amaurosis (LCA) to rod-cone dystrophy, also called retinitis pigmentosa (RP).
This meta-analysis suggests that the differential phenotype of patients with CRB1 mutations is due to additional modifying factors rather than particular mutant allele combination.

Photoreceptor dysplasia and degeneration in Crb1 mutants strongly vary with genetic background, suggesting that the variability in phenotypes of human patients that carry mutations in CRB1 may be due to interactions with background modifiers in addition to allelic variations.

nodes.description: Hyperopia (farsightedness) is noted in a substantial proportion of CRB1 patients, consistent with the shortened axial length sometimes associated with retinal dystrophies.

evidence.snippet: Hyperopia was noted for 6/11 patients including three for whom spherical equivalent was equal or above +5 diopters.

nodes.description: Keratoconus has been reported in CRB1 patients, suggesting CRB1 may have a role in corneal structural integrity beyond its retinal function.

evidence.snippet: Predisposition of the CRB1 patients to keratoconus

nodes.description: CRB1 localizes to the apical membrane of photoreceptors and Muller glial cells at the outer limiting membrane (OLM), where it maintains adherens junction integrity and cell polarity. Biallelic loss-of-function variants disrupt this structural scaffold, creating the shared proximal defect across the CRB1 disease family.
nodes.id: Loss of CRB1 at the outer limiting membrane

biological_processes: establishment or maintenance of cell polarity

evidence.snippet: Shortened photoreceptor inner and outer segments are observed as early as 2 weeks after birth, suggesting a developmental defect in these structures rather than a degenerative process.
CRB1 consists of 12 exons and exhibits alternative splicing at the 3' end, yielding two proteins of 1376 and 1406 amino acids

nodes.description: Central macular involvement with progressive macular atrophy, characterizing the macular dystrophy subtype but also occurring across other CRB1 subtypes.

evidence.snippet: 26 individuals were diagnosed with retinitis pigmentosa (RP; 25%), 54 with early-onset severe retinal dystrophy / Leber congenital amaurosis (EOSRD/LCA; 52%), and 24 with macular dystrophy (MD; 23%)
167_169 deletion was exclusively present in the MD cohort

nodes.description: Cystoid macular edema is found in approximately 50% of CRB1-RP patients, a higher prevalence than in overall RP, possibly related to vascular abnormalities or abnormal retinal lamination.

evidence.snippet: Cystoid fluid collections in the macula were found in 50% of RP patients.
Six of the patients displayed cystoid macular edema whereas the other five had macular thinning with loss of the outer retinal layers

nodes.description: Night blindness is a common presenting symptom in the RP branch, reflecting early rod photoreceptor dysfunction.

evidence.snippet: Night blindness was present in all patients but three, for whom a decrease of central vision and photophobia dominated.

nodes.description: Nystagmus is a characteristic sign of the severe EOSRD/LCA branch, reflecting early and severe visual impairment from birth or infancy.

evidence.snippet: The onset of the disease occurs at birth and the characteristic features include non-recordable electroretinogram (ERG), nystagmus, sluggish or absent pupillary responses and oculo-digital reflexes

nodes.description: Disrupted OLM integrity leads to abnormal retinal lamination with a thickened, immature-appearing retina. This structural disorganization affects both photoreceptor and Muller cell architecture and may have both developmental (congenital) and degenerative components.
nodes.id: Outer limiting membrane disruption and retinal disorganization

evidence.snippet: Photoreceptor degeneration is observed only within regions of retinal spotting, which is seen predominantly in the inferior nasal quadrant of the eye, and is caused by retinal folds and pseudorosettes.
The poor OCT lamination may have a degenerative component, as well as being congenital.

nodes.description: Progressive constriction of the peripheral visual field reflects ongoing rod photoreceptor loss in the RP branch.

evidence.snippet: The annual VF decline rate was 5% in patients from the genetic isolate, which was significantly faster than in non-GI patients (P < 0.05).

nodes.description: Pigmentary changes in CRB1-RP include either typical bone-spicule pigment migration or distinctive nummular (clumped) pigment deposits. Preserved para-arteriolar retinal pigment epithelium (PPRPE) is a hallmark but not universal finding.

evidence.snippet: 7/11 had typical bone spicule-shaped pigment migration within the peripheral retina whereas 4/11 had widespread clumped pigmentary changes of nummular appearance at the level of the retinal pigment epithelium

nodes.description: End-stage convergence across subtypes involves progressive rod and cone loss with declining visual acuity, visual field constriction, and extinguished electroretinogram. The rate of progression varies by subtype, with LCA/EOSRD showing earlier severe impairment than RP, and visual acuity survival analyses indicating an optimal intervention window in the first 2-3 decades.
nodes.id: Progressive photoreceptor degeneration and visual loss

evidence.snippet: Severe visual impairment was most frequent after 40 years of age for patients with RP and after 20 years of age for EOSRD/LCA.

In the RP group, median ages for reaching low vision, severe visual impairment, and blindness were 18, 32, and 44 years, respectively, with a visual acuity decline rate of 0.03 logarithm of the minimum angle of resolution per year.
Microperimetry showed a significant decrease in retinal sensitivity during follow-up and may be a more sensitive progression marker.

nodes.description: Progressive decline of visual acuity across all subtypes, with severity and rate depending on the CRB1 subtype. EOSRD/LCA patients experience earlier severe impairment than RP patients.

evidence.snippet: Longitudinal analysis revealed a significant difference between baseline and follow-up best-corrected visual acuity in the 3 subcohorts.

In the RP group, median ages for reaching low vision, severe visual impairment, and blindness were 18, 32, and 44 years, respectively, with a visual acuity decline rate of 0.03 logarithm of the minimum angle of resolution per year.

nodes.description: Non-recordable or severely attenuated electroretinogram is characteristic of advanced CRB1 disease, present from birth in LCA and developing progressively in RP.

evidence.snippet: Full-field electroretinography responses were extinguished in 50% of patients, were pathologically attenuated without a documented rod or cone predominance in 30% of patients, and showed a rod-cone dysfunction pattern in 20% of RP patients.
